# /product-research-ux-user-interview-research-brief

Build a research brief for user interview guides: sourced questions, findings, and implications. Produces a copy-ready research brief. Triggers: "user interview guides research-brief", /product-research-ux-user-interview-research-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this before a round of discovery interviews is recruited - when a team is about to spend two to four weeks of calendar time talking to customers and needs the sessions to actually change a decision. Typical decisions at stake: whether a problem is worth building for, which segment feels it most acutely, or why churned users left. If the team cannot name a decision the interviews would change, this brief will expose that before recruiting starts.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The product decision the interviews must inform, and its deadline
- Research questions: what the team needs to learn (these are not the questions asked to participants)
- Participant profile: the recent, observable behavior that qualifies someone (e.g. "switched tools in the last 90 days"), not demographics or job titles alone
- Prior evidence: past studies, support themes, analytics that already answer part of the question
- Session count that is feasible, and who moderates vs. takes notes

## Workflow

1. State the decision and its deadline in one sentence. If the decision would be made the same way regardless of what interviews reveal, say so plainly and recommend not running the study.
2. Convert the decision into 3-5 research questions ("What triggers users to look for an alternative?"), each traceable to the decision. Kill any question that is really a validation wish ("confirm users want feature X").
3. Write the screener around qualifying behavior, not attitudes: recency ("in the last 30 days"), frequency, and role in the workflow. Add disqualifiers for professional research participants and people with a stake in pleasing the team.
4. Draft the discussion guide: open with context-building about their actual workflow, then reconstruct the last specific time they did the task step by step. Ban hypothetical forms ("Would you use…?") and leading forms ("Don't you find X frustrating?"). Every question asks about past behavior or observable fact.
5. Add laddering probes under each core question - "What were you trying to get done?", "What did you do next?", "Why did that matter?" - to move from surface feature-talk to underlying motivation without suggesting answers.
6. Specify note-taking discipline: two-column notes separating verbatim observation ("she opened three tabs to compare") from interpretation ("comparison is effortful"), with the notetaker distinct from the moderator when possible.
7. State the analysis plan and its limits up front: 5-8 interviews per segment surface patterns and generate hypotheses; they do not measure prevalence. Write that sentence into the brief so stakeholders read it before fieldwork, not after.

## Output format

**Decision and deadline** - one sentence each.

**Research questions** - numbered, each mapped to the decision it informs.

**Participant screener** - qualifying behaviors, disqualifiers, target count per segment.

**Discussion guide** - opening context questions; timeline reconstruction of the last occurrence; laddering probes; closing catch-all ("What should I have asked about?").

**Prior evidence** - what existing data already answers, and the open gaps interviews must fill.

**Analysis plan and limits** - how findings map back to the decision; explicit statement of what this sample size can and cannot support.

## Do

- Ask about the last specific time something happened, never about habits in general or futures in hypothetical
- Keep research questions (team-facing) and interview questions (participant-facing) as separate lists
- Recruit on behavior; a title like "product manager" tells you almost nothing about workflow
- Mark which research questions prior evidence already answers, so sessions spend time only on true unknowns

## Don't

- Don't write questions that contain the team's preferred answer ("How much would faster export help you?")
- Don't let a stakeholder's feature idea appear anywhere in the guide as a concept to react to - that is a concept test, a different study
- Don't promise statistical conclusions from a qualitative sample, in the brief or in any follow-up framing
- Don't claim live workspace access or pull participant data without a successful connector/tool call, and don't skip approval before contacting real customers

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every guide question asks about past behavior or observable fact, not prediction or preference
- [ ] No question embeds the team's hypothesis as an assumption
- [ ] The screener qualifies by recent behavior, with disqualifiers listed
- [ ] The brief states in plain language what the planned N will and will not support
- [ ] A reader can trace each interview question up to a research question and down to the decision
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /product-research-ux-user-interview-research-brief or describe the task from the skill description.

```markdown
---

description: >
  Create a decision scorecard for user interview guides: a weighted comparison for choosing between options. Produces a copy-ready scorecard. Triggers: "user interview guides scorecard", /product-research-ux-user-interview-scorecard.
metadata:
  category: product

  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Product, Developer & Research
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /product-research-ux-user-interview-scorecard

Create a decision scorecard for user interview guides: a weighted comparison for choosing between options. Produces a copy-ready scorecard. Triggers: "user interview guides scorecard", /product-research-ux-user-interview-scorecard.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when interview evidence exists and the team must now choose between options - which problem to pursue, which segment to serve first, which concept to take into a usability test - and the risk is that the loudest advocate wins instead of the best-evidenced option. Also use it before fieldwork to choose between competing study designs (segments, recruiting channels, remote vs. in-person) when session budget is tight.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The options on the table, stated neutrally (no option named after its champion's framing)
- The insight report or raw evidence base, with per-insight confidence labels if they exist
- Decision criteria the team already argues about (user pain severity, frequency, reach, strategic fit, effort)
- Who decides, and the date the decision is needed

## Workflow

1. Fix the criteria and weights before looking at how any option scores. If weights are set after scores are visible, the scorecard becomes a rationalization device - note in the artifact that weights were pre-committed.
2. Split criteria into evidence-backed (scoreable from interview data: pain severity observed, workaround effort, frequency of the episode in participants' accounts) and judgment-based (strategic fit, build effort). Label each column accordingly; they are not the same kind of number.
3. For each evidence-backed cell, score only from tagged interview evidence and cite it: participant counts and a representative verbatim quote. A cell with no citable evidence gets "no data," not a guessed middle score - an honest gap beats a fabricated 3.
4. Attach a confidence label per cell (strong / emerging / anecdotal, inherited from the synthesis) so a high score on thin evidence is visibly different from a high score on consistent behavior across participants.
5. Compute weighted totals, then immediately stress-test: does the ranking flip if any anecdotal-confidence cell moves two points? If yes, report the ranking as unstable and name the specific evidence gap that would settle it.
6. Write the recommendation as a sentence a decision-maker can push back on: the leading option, the margin, the cells doing the work, and the cheapest piece of research that would de-risk the choice.

## Output format

**Decision** - what is being chosen, by whom, by when.

**Criteria and weights** - with a one-line rationale per weight and a note that weights were fixed before scoring.

**Scorecard table**

| Option | Criterion (weight) | Score | Confidence | Evidence (participant count + verbatim) |

**Weighted totals and sensitivity** - the ranking, plus whether it survives movement in low-confidence cells.

**Recommendation** - leading option, the evidence carrying it, and the follow-up research that would close the riskiest gap.

**Limits** - sample size behind the evidence columns and an explicit note that interview-derived scores rank options by observed signal, not by market-share prediction.

## Do

- Pre-commit weights and record that fact in the artifact
- Cite participant counts ("3 of 7 described this unprompted") inside cells, not just numbers
- Use "no data" as a legitimate cell value and list those cells as the study backlog
- Show the sensitivity check even when the ranking is stable - that is what makes the total trustworthy

## Don't

- Don't average away disagreement: if two scorers differ by 2+ points on a cell, record both scores and the reason, don't split the difference silently
- Don't let judgment-based columns (effort, strategy) borrow the credibility of the evidence-backed ones by formatting them identically without labels
- Don't present a weighted total from 6 interviews as if it measured demand; the scorecard ranks hypotheses
- Don't claim live workspace access or pull data without a successful connector/tool call, and don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Weights were set before scores and the artifact says so
- [ ] Every evidence-backed cell cites participant count and verbatim support, or says "no data"
- [ ] Each cell carries a confidence label
- [ ] A sensitivity note states whether the ranking survives low-confidence cells moving
- [ ] The recommendation names the follow-up research that would most change the outcome
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /product-research-ux-user-interview-scorecard or describe the task from the skill description.

```markdown
---
name: product-research-ux-user-interview-experiment-plan
description: >
  Design an experiment for user interview guides: a measurable test plan with success criteria. Produces a copy-ready experiment plan. Triggers: "user interview guides experiment-plan", /product-research-ux-user-interview-experiment-plan.
metadata:
  category: product
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Product, Developer & Research
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /product-research-ux-user-interview-experiment-plan

Design an experiment for user interview guides: a measurable test plan with success criteria. Produces a copy-ready experiment plan. Triggers: "user interview guides experiment-plan", /product-research-ux-user-interview-experiment-plan.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a team wants to treat an interview round as a falsifiable learning exercise rather than a listening tour - typically when there is a contested assumption ("SMB admins churn because setup is confusing") and stakeholders disagree about what customers would actually say. The decision at stake is usually whether to invest a build cycle based on that assumption. This skill turns the assumption into something interviews can genuinely disconfirm.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The riskiest assumption in plain words, and who on the team disagrees with it
- The decision that flips depending on whether the assumption survives
- Participant profile defined by recent behavior relevant to the assumption
- Prior evidence for and against the assumption (analytics, tickets, past research)
- Session budget and the deadline the decision cannot slip past

## Workflow

1. Write the assumption as a falsifiable statement about past behavior, not preference: "Most trial admins who churned attempted setup and abandoned it" is testable in interviews; "users want easier setup" is not.
2. Define disconfirming evidence before fieldwork: what participants would need to describe, unprompted, for the assumption to fail. If nothing a participant could say would count against it, the assumption is not being tested - flag this.
3. Set the evidence bar per session: the assumption gets support from a session only when the participant spontaneously reconstructs the behavior in a specific past episode - not when they agree with a summary the moderator offers.
4. Design the guide so the assumption is never voiced: approach it obliquely through timeline reconstruction of their last relevant episode, and only ladder deeper ("what did you do next?", "why?") - the moderator's job is to make it easy for the assumption to fail.
5. Plan the sample honestly: 5-8 sessions per segment detects whether a pattern exists at all. State the stop rule as saturation - stop when two consecutive sessions add no new behavior - and note that saturation signals pattern stability, not prevalence.
6. Pre-register the tally sheet: for each session, record supports / contradicts / silent on the assumption, with the verbatim quote or observed behavior that justified the tag. "Silent" is a valid and common outcome; forcing every session into support/contradict is how confirmation bias enters.
7. Define the readout rule in advance: e.g. "if 3+ of 6 churned-admin sessions contradict the assumption, we do not fund the setup redesign this quarter; we run a follow-up study on the alternative explanation." Tie the outcome to the decision, not to a report.

## Output format

**Assumption under test** - one falsifiable sentence, plus the decision that depends on it.

**Disconfirming evidence** - what a participant would describe, unprompted, for the assumption to fail.

**Session design** - participant profile, guide outline (timeline reconstruction, laddering probes), and the rule that the assumption is never spoken aloud.

**Tally sheet** - per-session supports / contradicts / silent, each with its verbatim evidence.

**Stop rule** - saturation definition and maximum session count.

**Decision mapping** - pre-committed readout: what the team does under each outcome, with the sample-size caveat written into the same paragraph.

## Do

- Make the disconfirmation condition concrete before the first session is scheduled
- Track "silent" sessions honestly; absence of mention is information about salience
- Quote verbatim evidence for every tally mark so a skeptic can re-audit the tags
- Present results as "pattern present/absent in this sample," never as a percentage of the market

## Don't

- Don't pitch the assumption to participants and count nods as evidence - agreement with a stated idea is near-worthless
- Don't extend fieldwork after a disappointing readout to "get a few more data points" unless the stop rule said so in advance
- Don't let five interviews be reported upstream as statistical proof; write the limitation into the artifact itself
- Don't claim live workspace access or fetch customer records without a successful connector/tool call, and don't contact real customers without approval

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The assumption is falsifiable and about past behavior
- [ ] Disconfirming evidence is defined before any session design
- [ ] The guide never states or hints at the assumption
- [ ] The stop rule and decision mapping were fixed before fieldwork, not after
- [ ] The plan states plainly that qualitative sessions establish existence of a pattern, not its prevalence
```

6. Pre-register the tally sheet: for each session, record supports / contradicts / silent on the assumption, with the verbatim quote or observed behavior that justified the tag. "Silent" is a valid and common outcome; forcing every session into support/contradict is how confirmation bias enters.
7. Define the readout rule in advance: e.g. "if 3+ of 6 churned-admin sessions contradict the assumption, we do not fund the setup redesign this quarter; we run a follow-up study on the alternative explanation." Tie the outcome to the decision, not to a report.

## Output format

**Assumption under test** - one falsifiable sentence, plus the decision that depends on it.

**Disconfirming evidence** - what a participant would describe, unprompted, for the assumption to fail.

**Session design** - participant profile, guide outline (timeline reconstruction, laddering probes), and the rule that the assumption is never spoken aloud.

**Tally sheet** - per-session supports / contradicts / silent, each with its verbatim evidence.

**Stop rule** - saturation definition and maximum session count.

**Decision mapping** - pre-committed readout: what the team does under each outcome, with the sample-size caveat written into the same paragraph.

## Do

- Make the disconfirmation condition concrete before the first session is scheduled
- Track "silent" sessions honestly; absence of mention is information about salience
- Quote verbatim evidence for every tally mark so a skeptic can re-audit the tags
- Present results as "pattern present/absent in this sample," never as a percentage of the market

## Don't

- Don't pitch the assumption to participants and count nods as evidence - agreement with a stated idea is near-worthless
- Don't extend fieldwork after a disappointing readout to "get a few more data points" unless the stop rule said so in advance
- Don't let five interviews be reported upstream as statistical proof; write the limitation into the artifact itself
- Don't claim live workspace access or fetch customer records without a successful connector/tool call, and don't contact real customers without approval

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The assumption is falsifiable and about past behavior
- [ ] Disconfirming evidence is defined before any session design
- [ ] The guide never states or hints at the assumption
- [ ] The stop rule and decision mapping were fixed before fieldwork, not after
- [ ] The plan states plainly that qualitative sessions establish existence of a pattern, not its prevalence

metadata:

allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /product-research-ux-user-interview-insight-report

Turn raw notes into insights for user interview guides: actionable patterns from messy inputs. Produces a copy-ready insight report. Triggers: "user interview guides insight-report", /product-research-ux-user-interview-insight-report.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this after fieldwork, when the team is sitting on transcripts, session recordings, or scrappy notes from a round of interviews and a decision meeting is coming. The failure mode this prevents: whoever synthesizes first anchors the team on their favorite quote, and the two interviews that contradicted the narrative quietly disappear. This produces synthesis a skeptical stakeholder can audit.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Raw material: transcripts, notes, or recordings, with participant IDs and segment labels
- The original research questions and the decision the study was meant to inform
- How many sessions were run, per segment, and any known recruiting compromises
- Any hypothesis the team held going in (needed to guard against confirmation bias, not to serve it)

## Workflow

1. Pass 1 - extract evidence atoms: go participant by participant and pull discrete observations, each as a verbatim quote or described behavior tagged with participant ID. One claim per atom. Do not interpret yet; "P4 exported to CSV then rebuilt the chart in Sheets" is an atom, "users don't trust our charts" is not.
2. Separate observation from interpretation structurally: keep two layers in the working document - the atom layer (what was said/done) and the inference layer (what it might mean). Every inference must cite the atom IDs it rests on.
3. Affinity-cluster the atoms bottom-up by behavioral similarity, not by which product area or team hypothesis they support. Name clusters with descriptive sentences ("People pre-clean data outside the tool before importing"), never with solution names.
4. Assign a confidence label per insight: **strong** (consistent across most participants and segments, behavior-based), **emerging** (multiple participants, or stated rather than demonstrated), **anecdotal** (one or two participants, or moderator-prompted). Base the label on evidence quality, not on how exciting the insight is.
5. Hunt contradicting evidence deliberately: for each insight, re-scan the atoms for participants who behaved otherwise, and record them under the insight ("P2 and P7 imported raw data without cleaning"). An insight with no listed exceptions from an honest scan of 6+ sessions is suspicious - either the scan was lazy or the claim is too vague to contradict.
6. Answer the original research questions explicitly, including "we still don't know" answers. Unanswered questions are a finding, and they feed the next study.
7. Write implications as options with owners ("worth a follow-up concept test on import flow - design"), not as decided roadmap items; interviews earn hypotheses, they do not close decisions alone.

## Output format

**Study snapshot** - sessions run per segment, method, recruiting caveats, dates.

**Insights** - one block each: descriptive-sentence headline; confidence label (strong / emerging / anecdotal) with one-line justification; supporting evidence (verbatim quotes with participant IDs); contradicting or absent evidence; interpretation clearly marked as inference.

**Research questions answered** - each original question with its answer or an honest "still open."

**Implications** - recommended next moves per insight, framed as options with suggested owners.

**Limits** - sample size, segments missing, and what this study cannot claim (e.g. "6 interviews; patterns here are hypotheses about existence, not measurements of prevalence").

## Do

- Quote participants verbatim with IDs so every insight is auditable back to its source
- Count and report participants per insight ("4 of 6") instead of "most users"
- Give contradicting evidence its own labeled line under each insight - presence of exceptions builds trust, not weakness
- Downgrade confidence when evidence is stated preference rather than demonstrated behavior

## Don't

- Don't cluster atoms by the team's pre-existing hypotheses - that is confirmation bias with sticky notes
- Don't let a vivid quote from one charismatic participant headline the report while quieter contradictions sit in an appendix
- Don't convert interview counts into percentages or present 5 interviews as statistical proof of anything
- Don't include participant names or identifying details in the shared report; use IDs, and don't claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every insight cites verbatim, participant-ID-tagged evidence
- [ ] Observation and interpretation are visibly separated in the artifact
- [ ] Each insight carries a strong/emerging/anecdotal label with justification
- [ ] Contradicting evidence is reported under the insights it challenges, not omitted
- [ ] The limits section states sample size and refuses prevalence claims in plain language
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /product-research-ux-user-interview-insight-report or describe the task from the skill description.

---
# /product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-research-brief

Build a research brief for JTBD synthesis: sourced questions, findings, and implications. Produces a copy-ready research brief. Triggers: "JTBD synthesis research-brief", /product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-research-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this before running Jobs-to-be-Done fieldwork - typically switch interviews with people who recently bought, subscribed, cancelled, or changed tools - when the team needs to understand what job customers hire the product for, and current segmentation by persona or firmographics is failing to explain behavior. Decisions at stake: positioning, onboarding focus, or which "job" the next quarter builds for.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The decision the job model must inform (positioning rewrite, roadmap bet, onboarding redesign)
- Access to recent switchers: people who made the buy/cancel/switch decision within roughly the last 90 days, while memory of the episode is still reconstructable
- Prior evidence: win/loss notes, cancellation reasons, sales call recordings that already hint at forces
- Interview budget and who moderates

## Workflow

1. Anchor on a switching event, not on product usage: JTBD evidence lives in the story of how someone moved from their old solution to a new one. Define which event the study reconstructs (first purchase, upgrade, cancellation) and recruit only people who actually made that move recently.
2. Write research questions in forces language: what pushed them away from the old way, what pulled them toward the new, what anxieties nearly stopped them, and what habits held them back. These four forces structure both the fieldwork and the later synthesis.
3. Design the interview as a timeline reconstruction run backwards from the decision: first thought, passive looking, active looking, deciding, first use. The guide's job is documentary - "walk me through the day you first thought 'I need something different'" - with concrete anchors (where were you, who else was involved, what did it cost you).
4. Ban category questions from the guide: "why did you choose us" invites rehearsed marketing-speak; "what were you using the Tuesday before you signed up, and what happened that week" gets the real story. Every question targets a specific remembered moment.
5. Plan the synthesis grammar in advance so notes are usable: job statements will be written as "When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [outcome]" - and notetakers should capture situation, motivation, and outcome elements verbatim as they appear, tagged by participant.
6. Set the sample and its limits: roughly 8-12 switch interviews around one switching event usually reach pattern saturation on the main forces; the brief must state that this yields a map of jobs and forces that exist, not their market distribution.
7. Define done: the brief names the artifact the fieldwork feeds (job statements with forces diagrams and evidence) and the decision meeting it must land before.

## Output format

**Decision and deadline** - what the job model will change.

**Switching event under study** - the specific move being reconstructed, and the recency window for recruits.

**Research questions** - organized by the four forces (push, pull, anxiety, habit).

**Interview guide** - timeline-reconstruction spine with moment-anchored questions and laddering probes; explicitly no "why us" questions.

**Recruiting plan** - switcher sources (recent wins, recent cancels, competitor switchers), screener behaviors, target count.

**Synthesis grammar and limits** - the job-statement format notes must feed, and the plain statement of what 8-12 interviews can support.

## Do

- Recruit on the switching event and its recency, not on being a current happy user
- Include cancellers and competitor-choosers, not only wins - the forces are clearest in the stories where you lost
- Capture the old solution in detail; the job is defined by what it replaced
- Keep the four-forces frame in the notes template so evidence lands pre-sorted

## Don't

- Don't interview people about a switch older than a few months and treat reconstructed haze as evidence
- Don't ask participants what job they are trying to do - the job is the researcher's synthesis, not the participant's vocabulary
- Don't blend feature-request gathering into switch interviews; it contaminates the timeline
- Don't claim access to CRM or call recordings without a successful connector/tool call, and get approval before contacting churned customers

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The study is anchored on a specific, recent switching event
- [ ] Research questions map to push/pull/anxiety/habit forces
- [ ] Every guide question targets a remembered moment, none ask "why did you choose us"
- [ ] The synthesis grammar (job-statement format) is defined before fieldwork
- [ ] The brief states what the planned interview count can and cannot claim
```

```markdown
---
name: product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-scorecard
description: >
  Create a decision scorecard for JTBD synthesis: a weighted comparison for choosing between options. Produces a copy-ready scorecard. Triggers: "JTBD synthesis scorecard", /product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-scorecard.
metadata:
  category: product
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Product, Developer & Research
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-scorecard

Create a decision scorecard for JTBD synthesis: a weighted comparison for choosing between options. Produces a copy-ready scorecard. Triggers: "JTBD synthesis scorecard", /product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-scorecard.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when JTBD synthesis has produced several candidate jobs and the team must pick which job to build for, position around, or onboard toward first. The trap this exists to counter: choosing the job that best matches what the team already wanted to build, then citing the research as cover. A scored comparison with visible evidence makes that move harder.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The candidate job statements with their forces evidence and confidence labels from synthesis
- The decision context: positioning, roadmap, or onboarding - the winning job differs by use
- Strategic constraints: segments the business must serve, capabilities it uniquely has
- Any behavioral or revenue data that can proxy job frequency or value (support themes, usage patterns, deal notes)

## Workflow

1. Fix criteria and weights before scoring, tuned to jobs rather than features. Defensible criteria: evidence strength (how well the job survived synthesis - participant spread, pool coverage), pain intensity (severity of the push forces observed), underservedness (how badly current alternatives, including yours, serve the job), strategic fit (can we win this job), and value proxy (deal/retention signals tied to the job). Freeze them, then score.
2. Split evidence-backed from judgment-based columns and label them. Evidence strength, pain intensity, and underservedness score from tagged interview atoms and must cite them; strategic fit is leadership judgment and says so; value proxy cites whatever operational data exists or reads "no data."
3. Score each evidence cell from the synthesis artifacts: participant counts per force, verbatim push quotes, described workarounds. A job with one gorgeous story scores low on evidence strength regardless of how well it fits the roadmap.
4. Carry confidence through: each row inherits its job's strong/emerging/anecdotal label, and totals for emerging/anecdotal jobs are displayed with a visible caveat, not formatted identically to strong ones.
5. Run the sensitivity pass on the top two: if swapping any judgment-based score within its plausible range flips the winner, report the choice as judgment-led, evidence-informed - that is legitimate, but it must be said out loud.
6. Recommend with a de-risking step attached: the leading job, the margin, and the cheapest validation experiment (message test, fake door, onboarding probe) that would confirm it before the organization reorients around it.

## Output format

**Decision** - what the winning job will drive, decided by whom, by when.

**Criteria and weights** - rationale per weight, evidence-backed vs. judgment-based labels, frozen-before-scoring note.

**Scorecard table**

| Job | Evidence strength | Pain intensity | Underservedness | Strategic fit (judgment) | Value proxy | Weighted total | Confidence |

**Evidence appendix** - per scored cell: participant counts, verbatim support, or "no data."

**Sensitivity** - whether the winner survives plausible movement in judgment cells.

**Recommendation** - leading job, margin, and the pre-commitment validation experiment before full reorientation.

## Do

- Score underservedness against all current alternatives including spreadsheets and manual workarounds, not just named competitors
- Let "no data" cells stand and list them as the research backlog
- Show emerging/anecdotal jobs in the table with degraded visual confidence, not excluded and not equal
- Attach the validation experiment to the recommendation as a condition, not an afterthought

## Don't

- Don't add a criterion mid-scoring because the favored job was losing - that is the exact failure this artifact exists to prevent
- Don't convert interview counts into market-size language ("the biggest job") - the scorecard ranks evidence and judgment, it does not size demand
- Don't let strategic fit, a judgment column, get the highest weight without stating that the ranking is therefore strategy-led
- Don't claim access to CRM or analytics without a successful connector/tool call, and don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Criteria and weights were frozen before any scores were entered, and the artifact says so

---
name: product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-scorecard
description: >
  Create a decision scorecard for JTBD synthesis: a weighted comparison for choosing between options. Produces a copy-ready scorecard. Triggers: "JTBD synthesis scorecard", /product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-scorecard.
metadata:
  category: product
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Product, Developer & Research
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-scorecard

Create a decision scorecard for JTBD synthesis: a weighted comparison for choosing between options. Produces a copy-ready scorecard. Triggers: "JTBD synthesis scorecard", /product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-scorecard.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when JTBD synthesis has produced several candidate jobs and the team must pick which job to build for, position around, or onboard toward first. The trap this exists to counter: choosing the job that best matches what the team already wanted to build, then citing the research as cover. A scored comparison with visible evidence makes that move harder.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The candidate job statements with their forces evidence and confidence labels from synthesis
- The decision context: positioning, roadmap, or onboarding - the winning job differs by use
- Strategic constraints: segments the business must serve, capabilities it uniquely has
- Any behavioral or revenue data that can proxy job frequency or value (support themes, usage patterns, deal notes)

## Workflow

1. Fix criteria and weights before scoring, tuned to jobs rather than features. Defensible criteria: evidence strength (how well the job survived synthesis - participant spread, pool coverage), pain intensity (severity of the push forces observed), underservedness (how badly current alternatives, including yours, serve the job), strategic fit (can we win this job), and value proxy (deal/retention signals tied to the job). Freeze them, then score.
2. Split evidence-backed from judgment-based columns and label them. Evidence strength, pain intensity, and underservedness score from tagged interview atoms and must cite them; strategic fit is leadership judgment and says so; value proxy cites whatever operational data exists or reads "no data."
3. Score each evidence cell from the synthesis artifacts: participant counts per force, verbatim push quotes, described workarounds. A job with one gorgeous story scores low on evidence strength regardless of how well it fits the roadmap.
4. Carry confidence through: each row inherits its job's strong/emerging/anecdotal label, and totals for emerging/anecdotal jobs are displayed with a visible caveat, not formatted identically to strong ones.
5. Run the sensitivity pass on the top two: if swapping any judgment-based score within its plausible range flips the winner, report the choice as judgment-led, evidence-informed - that is legitimate, but it must be said out loud.
6. Recommend with a de-risking step attached: the leading job, the margin, and the cheapest validation experiment (message test, fake door, onboarding probe) that would confirm it before the organization reorients around it.

## Output format

**Decision** - what the winning job will drive, decided by whom, by when.

**Criteria and weights** - rationale per weight, evidence-backed vs. judgment-based labels, frozen-before-scoring note.

**Scorecard table**

| Job | Evidence strength | Pain intensity | Underservedness | Strategic fit (judgment) | Value proxy | Weighted total | Confidence |

**Evidence appendix** - per scored cell: participant counts, verbatim support, or "no data."

**Sensitivity** - whether the winner survives plausible movement in judgment cells.

**Recommendation** - leading job, margin, and the pre-commitment validation experiment before full reorientation.

## Do

- Score underservedness against all current alternatives including spreadsheets and manual workarounds, not just named competitors
- Let "no data" cells stand and list them as the research backlog
- Show emerging/anecdotal jobs in the table with degraded visual confidence, not excluded and not equal
- Attach the validation experiment to the recommendation as a condition, not an afterthought

## Don't

- Don't add a criterion mid-scoring because the favored job was losing - that is the exact failure this artifact exists to prevent
- Don't convert interview counts into market-size language ("the biggest job") - the scorecard ranks evidence and judgment, it does not size demand
- Don't let strategic fit, a judgment column, get the highest weight without stating that the ranking is therefore strategy-led
- Don't claim access to CRM or analytics without a successful connector/tool call, and don't skip approval for sensitive actions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Criteria and weights were frozen before any scores were entered, and the artifact says so
- [ ] Evidence cells cite participant counts and verbatims; judgment cells are labeled
- [ ] Confidence labels from synthesis appear on every row
- [ ] Sensitivity of the winner to judgment cells is reported
- [ ] The recommendation includes a concrete validation step before organizational commitment

description: >
  Design an experiment for JTBD synthesis: a measurable test plan with success criteria. Produces a copy-ready experiment plan. Triggers: "JTBD synthesis experiment-plan", /product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-experiment-plan.
metadata:
  category: product
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Product, Developer & Research
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-experiment-plan

Design an experiment for JTBD synthesis: a measurable test plan with success criteria. Produces a copy-ready experiment plan. Triggers: "JTBD synthesis experiment-plan", /product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-experiment-plan.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this after JTBD synthesis has produced a candidate job statement - "When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [outcome]" - and before the company reorients positioning, onboarding, or a roadmap around it. A job model built from a dozen interviews is a hypothesis; this skill designs the cheapest test that could prove it wrong at scale before the rebrand or rebuild is funded.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The candidate job statement(s) and the forces evidence behind them (with interview counts)
- The decision waiting on validation: positioning rewrite, onboarding re-sequence, pricing repackaging
- Available test surfaces: landing pages, ad channels, onboarding flows, email segments, in-product prompts
- Baseline metrics on those surfaces (conversion, activation, click-through) to compare against
- Risk tolerance: what real-customer exposure is acceptable during the test

## Workflow

1. Pick the load-bearing claim inside the job statement. A job statement smuggles several claims: the situation is common, the motivation is the real driver (not a neighbor of it), and the outcome is what people would pay for. Identify which claim, if wrong, most breaks the pending decision - test that one.
2. Choose a test that exposes the claim to people who were not interviewed. The standard menu: message test (job-framed vs. current-framed landing page or ad copy, measured on click-through/signup), onboarding test (job-based first-run question routing users, measured on activation), or fake-door test (job-named entry point, measured on qualified clicks). Match the surface to where the decision will live.
3. Write the hypothesis with direction and metric: "Positioning the signup page around [job] outperforms current feature-led copy on visitor→trial conversion." Include the population the traffic represents - ad-channel visitors are not the customer base.
4. Design honest variants: the job-framed variant and the control must differ only in framing, not in polish, length, or offer. A prettier variant winning proves prettiness.
5. Set sample, duration, and stop rule appropriate to the surface: run until a pre-set minimum sample and duration (full weekly cycle at minimum) are met, decide against a pre-set threshold, and include a harm stop (kill the variant if conversion craters or support complaints spike). Avoid peeking-and-stopping on early noise.
6. Pre-commit interpretation, including the negative case: "if job-framed copy loses or ties, we do not rewrite positioning; we return to the interview evidence and test the next-ranked job." Also record what the test cannot say - a message test validates resonance of framing, not the full job model.
7. Log qualitative exhaust alongside the metric: on-page survey ("what were you hoping to get done today?"), support/chat mentions, sales-call echoes. These tell you whether the winning number won for the hypothesized reason.

## Output format

**Job statement under test** - with its interview-evidence summary and confidence level.

**Load-bearing claim** - the specific sub-claim being tested and why it carries the decision.

**Test design** - surface, variants (with the only-framing-differs constraint stated), population, assignment method.

**Metrics** - primary metric with baseline; guardrail metrics; qualitative exhaust channels.

**Sample, duration, stop rules** - pre-set minimums, decision threshold, harm stop.

**Decision mapping** - pre-committed actions for win / lose / tie, plus the explicit boundary of what this test validates.

## Do

- Test the job framing on people who were never interviewed - that is the entire point
- Keep variants identical except the framing under test
- Pair the quantitative readout with a "why" channel so a win is explainable
- State that a resonance win supports the job's framing, not every operational implication of the job model

## Don't

- Don't validate a job statement by showing it to the same interviewees and collecting nods
- Don't run the test on a traffic source that doesn't resemble the buyers the decision concerns, then generalize
- Don't stop the moment the variant pulls ahead; honor the pre-set sample and duration
- Don't expose pricing or commitments in a fake-door test without an approval gate, and don't claim live analytics access without a successful connector/tool call

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The tested claim is the one the pending decision actually rests on
- [ ] Variants differ only in job framing
- [ ] Sample minimum, duration, decision threshold, and harm stop are pre-committed
- [ ] The lose/tie action is written down before launch
- [ ] The plan states what the test validates and what it cannot, in one plain sentence
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-experiment-plan or describe the task from the skill description.

```markdown
---
name: product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-insight-report
description: >
  Turn raw notes into insights for JTBD synthesis: actionable patterns from messy inputs. Produces a copy-ready insight report. Triggers: "JTBD synthesis insight-report", /product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-insight-report.
metadata:
  category: product
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Product, Developer & Research
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-insight-report

Turn raw notes into insights for JTBD synthesis: actionable patterns from messy inputs. Produces a copy-ready insight report. Triggers: "JTBD synthesis insight-report", /product-research-ux-jtbd-synthesis-insight-report.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this after switch interviews (or a pile of win/loss notes, cancellation reasons, and sales-call transcripts) when the team needs the raw stories turned into a defensible job model: job statements, the forces acting on each switch, and hiring/firing criteria. The failure mode this prevents: "jobs" that are just feature requests wearing a trench coat, written to justify the existing roadmap.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Interview notes/transcripts with participant IDs and which switching event each reconstructs
- The decision the job model must inform, and its deadline
- Any pre-existing job or persona model the team holds (to check against, not to conform to)
- Sample facts: how many interviews, recruited from which pools (wins, cancels, competitor switchers)

## Workflow

1. Extract timeline atoms per participant: first thought, triggering event, old solution and its breakdown, alternatives considered, anxieties voiced, moment of commitment, first use. Each atom is verbatim or near-verbatim, tagged with participant ID. Where a participant's timeline has holes, record the hole - do not fill it with plausibility.
2. Sort atoms into the four forces per participant: push (what broke in the old way), pull (what attracted the new), anxiety (what nearly stopped them), habit (what kept them in place so long). A quote can only sit in one force per occurrence; force-shopping the same quote inflates whichever narrative is popular.
3. Cluster across participants by situation similarity, not by product feature: two people with the same triggering situation ("a client asked for something the old tool couldn't export") share a job candidate even if they praised different features.
4. Draft job statements per cluster in the format "When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [outcome]" - with each of the three slots filled only from atoms present in that cluster. If the motivation slot is guessed rather than evidenced, mark it as inference pending evidence.
5. Label confidence per job: **strong** (situation and motivation reconstructed independently by several participants across recruit pools), **emerging** (a few participants, or only one recruit pool - e.g. only wins, never cancels), **anecdotal** (one vivid story). Note that a job heard only from wins may be survivorship bias; the cancels tell you which jobs you failed.
6. Record contradictions and orphans: participants whose switch fits no cluster, and clusters where forces point in conflicting directions. Orphan stories are candidate jobs for a future round, not noise to delete.
7. Close the loop to the decision: for each strong job, state what it implies for the pending decision (positioning, onboarding, roadmap) as options - and list which jobs are too thin to act on without another study.

## Output format

**Corpus snapshot** - interviews per recruit pool, switching event covered, dates, known gaps.

**Job statements** - per job: the statement; confidence label with justification; forces diagram summary (push/pull/anxiety/habit with verbatim evidence and participant IDs per force); hiring criteria (what the new solution had to prove) and firing criteria (what got the old one fired).

**Contradictions and orphans** - unclustered stories and conflicting-force clusters, stated plainly.

**Implications** - decision-relevant options per strong job; explicit "do not act yet" list for emerging/anecdotal jobs.

**Limits** - interview count, pool skew (e.g. wins-heavy), and the standing note that this maps which jobs exist in the sample, not their market size.

## Do

- Fill every slot of a job statement from tagged evidence, and flag inferred slots visibly
- Keep separate evidence trails for push vs. pull - teams chronically overweight pull because it flatters the product
- Use cancel/loss interviews to test each job from the failure side
- Write jobs in the customer's situation language, free of your product's feature nouns

## Don't

- Don't reverse-engineer job statements from the roadmap and then decorate them with quotes
- Don't merge distinct situations into one mega-job to make the model look clean; three honest jobs beat one vague one
- Don't report how many participants expressed a job as evidence of market share - 12 switch interviews cannot size anything
- Don't include customer names or company identifiers in the shared report; use IDs, and don't claim CRM access without a successful connector/tool call

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every job statement's situation, motivation, and outcome trace to participant-ID-tagged atoms
- [ ] Each force in each diagram cites verbatim evidence
- [ ] Confidence labels reflect participant spread and recruit-pool coverage, not story vividness
- [ ] Orphan stories and contradictions appear in the report
- [ ] The limits section rules out market-sizing claims in plain language
```

---
# /product-research-ux-persona-reality-check-research-brief

Build a research brief for persona reality checks: sourced questions, findings, and implications. Produces a copy-ready research brief. Triggers: "persona reality checks research-brief", /product-research-ux-persona-reality-check-research-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a team's personas - "Marketing Mary," the slide with the stock photo - are steering real decisions (roadmap, onboarding paths, messaging) and nobody can say when they were last checked against evidence, or whether they were ever more than a workshop's guesses. The decision at stake: keep using the personas, repair them, or replace them with behavior-based segments. This brief plans the audit that answers it.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The persona artifacts themselves, with their provenance: who made them, when, from what data (if anyone remembers)
- The decisions currently being justified by persona ("we deprioritized X because Mary wouldn't use it")
- Available behavioral data: product usage by account/user attributes, subscription tiers, support history
- Access to real users matching each persona's supposed profile
- Who owns the personas politically - the audit will need their buy-in to change anything

## Workflow

1. Inventory the claims: decompose each persona into its individual factual claims - goals ("cares most about speed"), behaviors ("checks the dashboard daily"), pains, tool context, buying role. Number them. A persona is not true or false; its claims are, one by one.
2. Rank claims by load-bearing weight: which claims have actually driven decisions (from the decisions list), and which are decoration (age, hobbies, the stock photo). The audit spends its budget on load-bearing claims; decoration gets deleted, not researched.
3. Map each load-bearing claim to its cheapest adequate test: behavioral claims ("checks daily") test against usage data; goal and pain claims test in interviews with users matching the persona's profile; distribution claims ("most of our users are like Mary") test against account attributes at scale. Note claims that are untestable as written - vague claims get rewritten into testable form or flagged as unusable for decisions.
4. Plan the behavioral pass first: run the persona's supposed signature behaviors against actual usage cuts. Design the comparison to allow disconfirmation - including checking whether the claimed behavioral clusters exist at all, or whether usage clusters along entirely different lines (by workflow role, not by the persona axes).
5. Plan the interview pass second, seeded by the behavioral results: recruit real users from the segments the data shows (not from the persona template), reconstruct their actual goals and workflows with past-behavior questions, and note where their accounts match or break the persona's narrative. Around 5-8 interviews per data-visible segment; the brief states this supports claim-checking, not new statistical claims.
6. Pre-commit the verdict scheme: each audited claim will land as supported / contradicted / untested, with evidence attached; each persona then gets a disposition - keep (most load-bearing claims supported), repair (specific claims replaced), or retire (core claims contradicted or the underlying clusters don't exist). Committing the scheme now prevents the audit from softening into "directionally fine."
7. Set the political handling in the brief: findings go to the persona owner first with the evidence trail, and the replacement path (behavior-based segments from the clusters actually found) is proposed alongside any retirement - an audit that only demolishes gets shelved.

## Output format

**Persona inventory** - each persona, its provenance, and its numbered claims with load-bearing flags.

**Decisions at stake** - the real decisions persona claims have been driving.

**Claim-to-test map** - per load-bearing claim: the test (usage cut / interview theme / attribute distribution), the data needed, and rewritten versions of untestable claims.

**Fieldwork plan** - behavioral pass design including the cluster-existence check; interview pass with recruit-from-data rule and counts.

**Verdict scheme** - supported / contradicted / untested definitions, and the keep / repair / retire dispositions, pre-committed.

**Timeline and owner handling** - decision date, who sees findings first, and the replacement-path commitment.

## Do

- Audit claims, not vibes - the unit of verification is the individual assertion
- Spend the budget on claims that have driven decisions; delete decoration without ceremony
- Check whether the persona's clusters exist in usage data before checking the details
- Recruit interviewees from observed segments, not by casting for people who resemble the poster

## Don't

- Don't run a survey asking users which persona they identify with - that measures the poster's charm, not reality
- Don't let the audit test only the claims most likely to survive; the load-bearing ranking, not comfort, picks the targets
- Don't treat a founder's memory of early customers as evidence for current-user claims - provenance and freshness are part of the audit
- Don't pull user data without a successful connector/tool call, and get approval before interviewing customers

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every persona is decomposed into numbered, individually testable claims
- [ ] Load-bearing claims are identified from real decisions, and they get the research budget
- [ ] The behavioral pass includes a does-this-cluster-even-exist check
- [ ] The verdict scheme and dispositions are pre-committed
- [ ] The brief plans the replacement path, not just the demolition
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /product-research-ux-persona-reality-check-research-brief or describe the task from the skill description.

```markdown
---
name: product-research-ux-persona-reality-check-scorecard
description: >
  Create a decision scorecard for persona reality checks: a weighted comparison for choosing between options. Produces a copy-ready scorecard. Triggers: "persona reality checks scorecard", /product-research-ux-persona-reality-check-scorecard.
metadata:
  category: product
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Product, Developer & Research
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /product-research-ux-persona-reality-check-scorecard

Create a decision scorecard for persona reality checks: a weighted comparison for choosing between options. Produces a copy-ready scorecard. Triggers: "persona reality checks scorecard", /product-research-ux-persona-reality-check-scorecard.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The persona audit has run - some claims checked against usage data, some against interviews - and now each persona needs a verdict the team can act on: keep, repair, or retire. Use this to score the personas claim by claim against the evidence gathered, with every score carrying its evidence strength on its sleeve. The scorecard exists because "the audit felt mixed" is not a decision, and because without weighting, a persona can survive on ten correct decorative details while its one decision-driving claim lies dead.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Each persona's numbered claims, flagged load-bearing (has driven decisions) or decorative
- The evidence per claim: usage cuts, interview notes with participant counts, support data - whatever the audit produced
- Which decisions each load-bearing claim has been justifying
- The evidence provenance: when collected, from how many users/accounts, by what method
- Who owns the personas and will receive the verdicts

## Workflow

1. Score claims, not personas: each numbered claim gets a verdict - supported, contradicted, or untested - assigned strictly from the evidence on file. A claim with no evidence is untested, never "probably fine"; the scorecard's integrity lives or dies on refusing to fill gaps with plausibility.
2. Attach an evidence-strength label to every verdict: **strong** (behavioral data at scale, or consistent across most interview participants and corroborated elsewhere), **emerging** (a repeated pattern in a small sample, one data cut, direction consistent but thin), **anecdotal** (one or two mentions, a stakeholder memory, a single vivid quote). A "supported (anecdotal)" claim is a hypothesis wearing a checkmark - the label is what stops it from being treated as settled.
3. Record the observation separately from the reading on every scored row: what the evidence literally showed ("4 of 6 interviewed admins described delegating report setup"; "the daily-dashboard cohort is 8% of matching accounts") versus what the scorer concludes ("the 'hands-on Mary' claim is contradicted"). Reviewers must be able to disagree with the reading while accepting the observation.
4. Weight by load-bearing status before totaling: a persona's verdict is driven by its decision-driving claims - a persona with supported decoration and a contradicted core claim fails, whatever its average. State the weights in the open (which claims counted double or more, and why).
5. Map weighted results to dispositions using pre-agreed thresholds: **keep** (load-bearing claims supported at strong or emerging), **repair** (specific load-bearing claims contradicted or untested - list exactly which, and what evidence would settle each), **retire** (core claims contradicted at strength, or the underlying segment doesn't appear in the data at all). Never average a contradicted core into a passing grade.
6. State the limits box before the verdicts ship: interview n and recruitment source, data window, segments not covered, and the standing caveat that a handful of interviews can contradict a universal claim but cannot certify one. Then hand the owner the sheet with contradicting evidence displayed, not summarized away.

## Output format

**Verdict summary** - per persona: keep / repair / retire, one line of deciding rationale each

**Claim scorecard**

| # | Claim | Load-bearing? | Verdict | Evidence strength | Observation (what the evidence showed) | Reading (what we conclude) |

**Weighting note** - which claims counted more and why

**Repair list** - per repaired persona: the failed claims, and the evidence that would settle each

**Limits box** - n, recruitment source, data window, uncovered segments, small-sample caveat verbatim

**Contradicting evidence** - anything that cuts against the verdicts, shown, not smoothed

## Do

- Give every verdict an evidence-strength label; an unlabeled score is an opinion in a table
- Keep observation and reading in separate columns so disputes attach to the right one
- Let one contradicted load-bearing claim outweigh any amount of supported decoration
- Mark evidence gaps as untested and say what test would close each

## Don't

- Don't average scores into a persona-level number that lets a dead core claim hide
- Don't upgrade anecdotal to emerging because the anecdote was memorable or came from an executive
- Don't present interview counts as proportions of the user base - 4 of 6 participants is 4 of 6 participants, nothing more
- Don't delete the contradicting-evidence section when it complicates a clean verdict; that section is the audit
- Don't copy external templates or claim live workspace access without a successful connector/tool call
- Don't pull user data or share customer quotes outside the team without a successful connector/tool call and explicit approval

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every claim row has a verdict, a strength label, and split observation/reading columns
- [ ] Weights are explicit and load-bearing claims dominate the disposition
- [ ] No untested claim is scored as supported
- [ ] The limits box states n, sources, and the small-sample caveat
- [ ] Contradicting evidence is displayed, not summarized away
- [ ] Any connector or high-stakes action has an approval gate
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /product-research-ux-persona-reality-check-scorecard or describe the task from the skill description.

```markdown
---
name: product-research-ux-persona-reality-check-experiment-plan
description: >
  Design an experiment for persona reality checks: a measurable test plan with success criteria. Produces a copy-ready experiment plan. Triggers: "persona reality checks experiment-plan", /product-research-ux-persona-reality-check-experiment-plan.
metadata:
  category: product
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
  real_world_use_case: Product, Developer & Research
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /product-research-ux-persona-reality-check-experiment-plan

Design an experiment for persona reality checks: a measurable test plan with success criteria. Produces a copy-ready experiment plan. Triggers: "persona reality checks experiment-plan", /product-research-ux-persona-reality-check-experiment-plan.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The team suspects its personas are fiction, but "we should do some research" keeps stalling because nobody has framed a test that could actually settle it. Use this to design that test: persona claims converted into falsifiable predictions, run against behavioral data plus a small structured interview set, with pass/fail criteria committed before anyone sees a result. The output is a plan the persona's defenders would accept as fair in advance - which is what makes the verdict stick afterward.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The personas and the specific claims they make: behaviors, goals, pains, implied segment sizes
- Which claims are currently driving decisions - those are the only ones worth spending the test on
- Available behavioral data: product events, account attributes, plan tiers, support history
- Interview access: how many real users can realistically be recruited inside the decision window
- The decision these results feed, and its date

## Workflow

1. Convert each load-bearing persona claim into a prediction that data could contradict: "Mary checks the dashboard daily" becomes "among users matching Mary's profile filters, median dashboard sessions meet or exceed N per week." Any claim that can't be phrased as a losable bet is flagged untestable and barred from justifying decisions until rewritten.
2. Pre-register the thresholds: for every prediction, write down - before analysis - what result counts as supported, contradicted, or inconclusive. Thresholds set after seeing data always drift toward whatever the data showed.
3. Design the behavioral arm first: define the profile filters that operationalize each persona, run the predictions against usage cuts, and include the null test - cluster the usage data with no persona labels and check whether persona-shaped groups appear at all, or whether behavior clusters on entirely different axes (workflow role, team size, tenure).
4. Design the interview arm for what logs can't see - goals, pains, buying context: 5-8 users per data-visible segment, recruited from observed behavior, never by resemblance to the persona poster. Every question targets the last specific time, not the general case ("walk me through the last time you opened the dashboard - what were you trying to decide?"); asking what someone like Mary would want is precisely the fiction under audit.
5. Keep the evidence ledger separate from the verdicts: record observations (what the data showed, what a participant said and did) apart from interpretations (what the team believes it means). Every interview finding carries its n. State in the plan itself: five to eight interviews can kill a claim by counterexample or surface doubt - they cannot statistically confirm one.
6. Set the stop rule and disposition mapping: analysis ends at the decision date; each claim lands supported / contradicted / untested; each persona then lands keep / repair / retire based on how its load-bearing claims fared against the pre-registered thresholds - and nothing else.

## Output format

**Hypotheses table** - persona claim → falsifiable prediction → data source → pre-registered threshold

**Behavioral arm** - profile filters, usage cuts, and the unlabeled-clustering null test

**Interview arm** - recruit-from-behavior rule, per-segment counts, past-behavior question guide

**Evidence rules** - observation vs. interpretation logged separately; n stated on every interview finding; explicit note of what small-n evidence can and cannot conclude

**Verdict scheme** - supported / contradicted / untested criteria and the keep / repair / retire mapping

**Timeline and stop rule** - analysis window, decision date, owner

## Do

- Phrase every hypothesis so the persona's defenders agree beforehand on what would disprove it
- Run the does-this-cluster-even-exist test before testing any cluster's details
- Recruit interviewees from observed behavior, not from casting calls for people who match the poster
- Attach sample size and stated limits to every interview-sourced finding

## Don't

- Don't test only the claims most likely to survive - the decision-driving claims pick themselves
- Don't present a handful of interviews as confirmation of a segment-level claim; small n disconfirms and suggests, it never proves
- Don't move thresholds after seeing results - inconclusive is an allowed and honest outcome
- Don't blend what participants said with what the team concluded; the ledger keeps them apart
- Don't pull user data or contact customers without a successful connector/tool call and explicit approval first

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every hypothesis is phrased as a bet the persona could lose
- [ ] Thresholds are pre-registered before any analysis begins
- [ ] The null cluster-existence test is in the behavioral arm
- [ ] Observations and interpretations have separate homes in the evidence ledger
- [ ] Every interview finding states its n and what it cannot claim
- [ ] Data access and customer contact have explicit approval gates
```

allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /product-research-ux-persona-reality-check-experiment-plan

Design an experiment for persona reality checks: a measurable test plan with success criteria. Produces a copy-ready experiment plan. Triggers: "persona reality checks experiment-plan", /product-research-ux-persona-reality-check-experiment-plan.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The team suspects its personas are fiction, but "we should do some research" keeps stalling because nobody has framed a test that could actually settle it. Use this to design that test: persona claims converted into falsifiable predictions, run against behavioral data plus a small structured interview set, with pass/fail criteria committed before anyone sees a result. The output is a plan the persona's defenders would accept as fair in advance - which is what makes the verdict stick afterward.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The personas and the specific claims they make: behaviors, goals, pains, implied segment sizes
- Which claims are currently driving decisions - those are the only ones worth spending the test on
- Available behavioral data: product events, account attributes, plan tiers, support history
- Interview access: how many real users can realistically be recruited inside the decision window
- The decision these results feed, and its date

## Workflow

1. Convert each load-bearing persona claim into a prediction that data could contradict: "Mary checks the dashboard daily" becomes "among users matching Mary's profile filters, median dashboard sessions meet or exceed N per week." Any claim that can't be phrased as a losable bet is flagged untestable and barred from justifying decisions until rewritten.
2. Pre-register the thresholds: for every prediction, write down - before analysis - what result counts as supported, contradicted, or inconclusive. Thresholds set after seeing data always drift toward whatever the data showed.
3. Design the behavioral arm first: define the profile filters that operationalize each persona, run the predictions against usage cuts, and include the null test - cluster the usage data with no persona labels and check whether persona-shaped groups appear at all, or whether behavior clusters on entirely different axes (workflow role, team size, tenure).
4. Design the interview arm for what logs can't see - goals, pains, buying context: 5-8 users per data-visible segment, recruited from observed behavior, never by resemblance to the persona poster. Every question targets the last specific time, not the general case ("walk me through the last time you opened the dashboard - what were you trying to decide?"); asking what someone like Mary would want is precisely the fiction under audit.
5. Keep the evidence ledger separate from the verdicts: record observations (what the data showed, what a participant said and did) apart from interpretations (what the team believes it means). Every interview finding carries its n. State in the plan itself: five to eight interviews can kill a claim by counterexample or surface doubt - they cannot statistically confirm one.
6. Set the stop rule and disposition mapping: analysis ends at the decision date; each claim lands supported / contradicted / untested; each persona then lands keep / repair / retire based on how its load-bearing claims fared against the pre-registered thresholds - and nothing else.

## Output format

**Hypotheses table** - persona claim → falsifiable prediction → data source → pre-registered threshold

**Behavioral arm** - profile filters, usage cuts, and the unlabeled-clustering null test

**Interview arm** - recruit-from-behavior rule, per-segment counts, past-behavior question guide

**Evidence rules** - observation vs. interpretation logged separately; n stated on every interview finding; explicit note of what small-n evidence can and cannot conclude

**Verdict scheme** - supported / contradicted / untested criteria and the keep / repair / retire mapping

**Timeline and stop rule** - analysis window, decision date, owner

## Do

- Phrase every hypothesis so the persona's defenders agree beforehand on what would disprove it
- Run the does-this-cluster-even-exist test before testing any cluster's details
- Recruit interviewees from observed behavior, not from casting calls for people who match the poster
- Attach sample size and stated limits to every interview-sourced finding

## Don't

- Don't test only the claims most likely to survive - the decision-driving claims pick themselves
- Don't present a handful of interviews as confirmation of a segment-level claim; small n disconfirms and suggests, it never proves
- Don't move thresholds after seeing results - inconclusive is an allowed and honest outcome
- Don't blend what participants said with what the team concluded; the ledger keeps them apart
- Don't pull user data or contact customers without a successful connector/tool call and explicit approval first

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every hypothesis is phrased as a bet the persona could lose
- [ ] Thresholds are pre-registered before any analysis begins
- [ ] The null cluster-existence test is in the behavioral arm
- [ ] Observations and interpretations have separate homes in the evidence ledger
- [ ] Every interview finding states its n and what it cannot claim
- [ ] Data access and customer contact have explicit approval gates
